ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

Downstream writers will use the query_postgres tool to inspect source data while implementing your plan.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 19, 59, 21, 640533, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 19, 59, 21, 640543, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 19, 59, 21, 640673, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3903-8c27-7527-a40d-2cc4fccd124e', conversation_id='019f3903-8c27-7527-a40d-2cc3ffe059fd')

data.overview: The global mapping plan addresses the transformation of source data from `fixture_master_v2_src` into the target Salesforce-like schema. Key generation will use MD5 hashes of source natural keys to ensure uniqueness and consistency for target `Id` fields. All foreign key relationships (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) will consistently use these generated MD5-hashed IDs. Legacy ID fields (`Legacy_*__c`) will directly store the source system's natural key. Date parsing will handle multiple formats (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`) and output `YYYY-MM-DD`, with unparseable dates becoming NULL. Amount parsing will clean currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Enum fields will use explicit CASE statements for mapping, falling back to NULL for unrecognised values. Default values will be applied for `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate` (using `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`), and `IsDeleted` (defaulting to `0`). All string fields will be `TRIM`med.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 0, 44, 701819, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 0, 44, 702153, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: The global mapping plan addresses the transformation of source data from `fixture_master_v2_src` into the target Salesforce-like schema. Key generation will use MD5 hashes of source natural keys to ensure uniqueness and consistency for target `Id` fields. All foreign key relationships (`AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) will consistently use these generated MD5-hashed IDs. Legacy ID fields (`Legacy_*__c`) will directly store the source system's natural key. Date parsing will handle multiple formats (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`) and output `YYYY-MM-DD`, with unparseable dates becoming NULL. Amount parsing will clean currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Enum fields will use explicit CASE statements for mapping, falling back to NULL for unrecognised values. Default values will be applied for `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate` (using `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`), and `IsDeleted` (defaulting to `0`). All string fields will be `TRIM`med.
Source tables: fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities, fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden
Join keys: master_opportunities.kunden_ref = master_kunden.kundennummer
- Id <- opp_kennung (Generate UUID/MD5 from `opp_kennung`)
- Name <- titel (TRIM(`titel`) (NOT NULL))
- StageName <- vertriebsphase (CASE LOWER(`vertriebsphase`) WHEN 'interessentenphase' THEN 'Prospecting' WHEN 'qualifizierung' THEN 'Qualification' WHEN 'bedarfsanalyse' THEN 'Needs Analysis' WHEN 'wertangebot' THEN 'Value Proposition' WHEN 'entscheider identifiziert' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers' WHEN 'wahrnehmungsanalyse' THEN 'Perception Analysis' WHEN 'angebot/preisverhandlung' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote' WHEN 'verhandlung/pruefung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review' WHEN 'gewonnen' THEN 'Closed Won' WHEN 'verloren' THEN 'Closed Lost' ELSE NULL END (NOT NULL))
- CloseDate <- zieldatum (Parse `zieldatum` (text) to YYYY-MM-DD. Handle 'DD.MM.YYYY', 'YYYYMMDD', 'MM/DD/YYYY'. Prefer NULL for unparseable. (NOT NULL))
- Amount <- auftragswert (Clean `auftragswert` (e.g., remove currency symbols, handle ',' as decimal, '.' as thousand separator) and cast to DOUBLE PRECISION. E.g., `REPLACE(REPLACE(auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION`)
- CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode (TRIM(UPPER(`waehrungscode`)))
- AccountId <- kunden_ref (Generate UUID/MD5 from `master_kunden.kundennummer` using `master_opportunities.kunden_ref` for join.)
- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung (Direct map `opp_kennung`)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Default to CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Default to CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0)

Follow the plan. Produce the dbt model for `Opportunity`.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 0, 44, 702155, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 0, 44, 702783, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3904-d097-7326-b132-a4d8a8ad15fa', conversation_id='019f3904-d097-7326-b132-a4d708a5ddfd')

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args={'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(opp.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    TRIM(opp.titel) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'interessentenphase' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'qualifizierung' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'bedarfsanalyse' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'wertangebot' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'entscheider identifiziert' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'wahrnehmungsanalyse' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'angebot/preisverhandlung' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'verhandlung/pruefung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'gewonnen' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'verloren' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    REPLACE(REPLACE(opp.auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opp.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(kunde.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opp.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opp
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunde
    ON opp.kunden_ref = kunde.kundennummer

Unmatched values default to NULL.', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.vertriebsphase'], 'target_column': 'StageName'}, {'target_column': 'CloseDate', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.zieldatum'], 'explanation': 'Parsed from the source date string, handling multiple formats (DD.MM.YYYY, YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY) and formatted as YYYY-MM-DD. Unparseable dates default to NULL.'}, {'explanation': "Cleaned by removing thousand separators ('.') and replacing decimal commas (',') with dots ('.') before casting to DOUBLE PRECISION.", 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.auftragswert'], 'target_column': 'Amount'}, {'target_column': 'CurrencyIsoCode', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.waehrungscode'], 'explanation': 'Mapped from the source currency code, trimmed and converted to uppercase.'}, {'target_column': 'AccountId', 'explanation': 'Generated by MD5 hashing the customer number from the `master_kunden` table, joined via `kunden_ref`.', 'source_columns': ['master_kunden.kundennummer', 'master_opportunities.kunden_ref']}, {'target_column': 'Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c', 'explanation': 'Directly mapped from the source opportunity identifier for legacy tracking.', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.opp_kennung']}, {'explanation': 'Defaults to the current timestamp as a text string.', 'source_columns': [], 'target_column': 'CreatedDate'}, {'target_column': 'LastModifiedDate', 'source_columns': [], 'explanation': 'Defaults to the current timestamp as a text string.'}, {'target_column': 'IsDeleted', 'explanation': 'Defaults to 0, indicating the record is not deleted.', 'source_columns': []}], 'reasoning': 'This model transforms raw opportunity data into the target Opportunity schema by joining master_opportunities with master_kunden. ```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(opp.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    TRIM(opp.titel) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'interessentenphase' THEN 'Prospecting'

When fixing errors:

- Ensure every mixed-case output column uses a double-quoted alias: `expr AS "ColumnName"`.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(opp.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    TRIM(opp.titel) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'interessentenphase' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'qualifizierung' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'bedarfsanalyse' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'wertangebot' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'entscheider identifiziert' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'wahrnehmungsanalyse' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'angebot/preisverhandlung' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'verhandlung/pruefung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'gewonnen' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(opp.vertriebsphase) = 'verloren' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN opp.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opp.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    REPLACE(REPLACE(opp.auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opp.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(kunde.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opp.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opp
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunde
    ON opp.kunden_ref = kunde.kundennummer

```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 1, 40, 543259, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 1, 40, 543259, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Defaults to '1900-01-01' if unparseable or null.", 'target_column': 'CloseDate', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.zieldatum']}, {'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.auftragswert'], 'target_column': 'Amount', 'explanation': "Cleans and casts the monetary amount to a double precision number, handling 'EUR ' prefix and 'None' string values."}, {'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.waehrungscode'], 'explanation': 'Normalizes various source currency codes and symbols to standard ISO 4217 currency codes.', 'target_column': 'CurrencyIsoCode'}, {'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.kunden_ref'], 'target_column': 'AccountId', 'explanation': 'References the Account ID, which corresponds to the customer reference in the source.'}, {'target_column': 'Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c', 'explanation': 'Stores the original opportunity identifier for legacy tracking.', 'source_columns': ['master_opportunities.opp_kennung']}, {'target_column': 'CreatedDate', 'explanation': 'Defaults to the current date as no source mapping is available.', 'source_columns': []}, {'source_columns': [], 'target_column': 'LastModifiedDate', 'explanation': 'Defaults to the current date as no source mapping is available.'}, {'source_columns': [], 'explanation': 'Defaults to 0 (false) as no source mapping is available.', 'target_column': 'IsDeleted'}], 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Id",
    COALESCE(mo.titel, 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen', 'closed won') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate (zieldatum)
    CASE
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN mo.zieldatum
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate if unparseable
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount (auftragswert)
    CASE
        WHEN mo.auftragswert IS NULL OR mo.auftragswert = 'None' THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(REPLACE(mo.auftragswert, 'EUR ', '') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode (waehrungscode)
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('euro', '€', 'eur') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    mo.kunden_ref AS "AccountId", -- AccountId is the kundennummer from master_kunden, which is kunden_ref here.
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS mo

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

- target_table: name of the model

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Id",
    COALESCE(mo.titel, 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate (zieldatum)
    CASE
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN mo.zieldatum
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate if unparseable, to satisfy NOT NULL constraint
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount (auftragswert) - handle European number format
    CASE
        WHEN mo.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(mo.auftragswert) IN ('None', '') THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(mo.auftragswert, '[^0-9\.,]', '', 'g')), '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode (waehrungscode)
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('euro', '€', 'eur') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    mo.kunden_ref AS "AccountId",
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS mo

```

  - Amount <- master_opportunities.auftragswert: Transforms the `auftragswert` by removing non-numeric characters (except dot and comma), then handling European number formats (removing thousand-separator dots and replacing decimal commas with dots) before casting to DOUBLE PRECISION. Null if source is null or empty.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- master_opportunities.waehrungscode: Maps source currency codes to standard ISO currency codes, or NULL if not recognized.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 3, 15, 348959, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Id",
    COALESCE(mo.titel, 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate (zieldatum)
    CASE
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN mo.zieldatum
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate if unparseable, to satisfy NOT NULL constraint
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount (auftragswert) - handle European number format
    CASE
        WHEN mo.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(mo.auftragswert) IN ('None', '') THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(mo.auftragswert, '[^0-9\.,]', '', 'g')), '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode (waehrungscode)
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('euro', '€', 'eur') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    mo.kunden_ref AS "AccountId",
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS mo

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- master_opportunities.opp_kennung: Directly mapped from the opportunity unique identifier.
  - Name <- master_opportunities.titel: Opportunity name, defaulting to 'Unknown Opportunity' if null.
  - StageName <- master_opportunities.vertriebsphase: Mapped from source sales phase to Salesforce-compatible StageName enum, defaulting to 'Prospecting'.
  - CloseDate <- master_opportunities.zieldatum: Parses various date formats from the source `zieldatum` to 'YYYY-MM-DD', defaulting to '1900-01-01' for unparseable values to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - Amount <- master_opportunities.auftragswert: Transforms the `auftragswert` by removing non-numeric characters (except dot and comma), then handling European number formats (removing thousand-separator dots and replacing decimal commas with dots) before casting to DOUBLE PRECISION. Null if source is null or empty.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- master_opportunities.waehrungscode: Maps source currency codes to standard ISO currency codes, or NULL if not recognized.
  - AccountId <- master_opportunities.kunden_ref: References the customer ID, assumed to be the Salesforce-style Account ID.
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- master_opportunities.opp_kennung: The original opportunity identifier from the source system.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to the current date.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to the current date.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Hardcoded to 0, indicating not deleted.
Reasoning: The primary fix addresses the `invalid input syntax for type double precision` error by implementing explicit logic for European number formats in the `Amount` field. It now removes non-numeric characters, then removes thousand-separator dots and replaces the decimal comma with a dot before casting. The `CloseDate` default for unparseable dates is retained as `1900-01-01` to meet the NOT NULL constraint, despite a general preference for NULLs over sentinels.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 3, 15, 348959, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

# Transformation guidelines

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM

  - IsDeleted <- (none): Hardcoded to 0 as a default value.

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Id",
    COALESCE(mo.titel, 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('won', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('qualifikation', 'quali', 'qualification') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt', 'prospect') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.vertriebsphase) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate (zieldatum)
    CASE
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN mo.zieldatum
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN mo.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(mo.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE '1900-01-01' -- Default for NOT NULL CloseDate if unparseable, to satisfy NOT NULL constraint
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount (auftragswert) - handle European number format
    CASE
        WHEN mo.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(mo.auftragswert) IN ('None', '') THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(mo.auftragswert, '[^0-9\.,]', '', 'g')), '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode (waehrungscode)
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('euro', '€', 'eur') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(mo.waehrungscode) IN ('chf') THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    mo.kunden_ref AS "AccountId",
    mo.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_DATE::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS mo

```

  - Id <- master_opportunities.opp_kennung: Directly mapped from the opportunity unique identifier.

  - Amount <- master_opportunities.auftragswert: Transforms the `auftragswert` by removing non-numeric characters (except dot and comma), then handling European number formats (removing thousand-separator dots and replacing decimal commas with dots) before casting to DOUBLE PRECISION. Null if source is null or empty.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 3, 15, 348959, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database
